findings.claim: Single-segment parent scope '*' grants unrestricted authority to any child scope — a wildcard grant equivalent to '*:*:*' that the auditor brief explicitly calls out as prohibited.
findings.detail: The attenuation algorithm zips parent_segments with child_segments and, for any parent segment equal to '*', accepts unconditionally. A parent scope of '*' has parent_segments == ['*'] (length 1). The zip iterates exactly one pair: ('*', child[0]) which passes. Because the loop only iterates over parent's length, any suffix child segments are NEVER examined. Verified by fuzzer: parent='*' child='store:post:write' returns Ok. This means a capability with scope '*' permits literally every operation in the system. The auditor success metric 'Zero capability grants with wildcard scope *:* (too broad)' is violated by a broader single-'*' bypass. Worse: GrantScope::parse('*') succeeds (non-empty after trim), so such a scope is fully constructible through the public API.
findings.suggested_fix: (a) Reject single-segment '*' scopes (and any scope whose non-wildcard segment count is zero) at GrantScope::parse. (b) Change the algorithm to require that when parent has fewer segments than child, the parent's final segment MUST be '*' (namespace-prefix), otherwise attenuation fires. (c) Even with (b), decide explicitly whether '*:*' grants the universe or just a 2-segment subtree. Add a proptest `prop_parent_star_does_not_grant_more_than_parent_intended` with at least 10_000 random chain generations.

findings.claim: Parent scope with trailing wildcard (e.g. 'store:*') permits children that extend to arbitrary depth, fabricating action segments the parent never authorized.
findings.detail: Because the zip iterates over parent's length only, any child segments beyond the parent's length are never examined. Fuzzer confirms: parent='store:*' child='store:anything:write:delete' returns Ok. If scope semantics follow the standard UCAN/namespace convention resource:action:qualifier, then a child claiming 'store:anything:write:delete' has fabricated both a 'write' action and a 'delete' qualifier that the parent 'store:*' never granted. The positive-boundary test in call_attenuation.rs (parent='store:post:*', child='store:post:read') masks this because the child there has the same depth as the parent plus one. A child-DEEPER-than-parent test that exercises the bypass does not exist. This is precisely the 'delegation chain attack' threat C3 — 'child scope that claims to narrow a parent scope may widen it via wildcard abuse'.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the attenuation check structural, not textual: (a) only a TRAILING single '*' in parent is namespace-permissive for deeper children; intermediate '*' must match exactly one child segment. (b) If parent has no trailing '*' and len(parent) < len(child), the child is claiming authority beyond the parent and must attenuate unless parent is a pure hierarchical prefix with an explicit policy. (c) Add property tests: for N in 2..10, random chain of N scopes each claiming to narrow the last — assert effective scope is a structural subset of EVERY ancestor in the chain, not just the immediate parent.

findings.claim: GrantScope::parse accepts scopes with empty colon-separated segments (e.g. 'store::write', ':::', 'a::b') that the attenuation algorithm then mishandles unpredictably.
findings.detail: Parse only checks trimmed-nonempty at whole-string level. Fuzzer confirms ':::' parses successfully and splits to ['','','',''] in check_attenuation. This is a canonicalization gap: 'store::post' vs 'store:post' produce different CIDs (different byte content) but may be intended to mean the same thing, violating Invariant 10's content-addressing clarity. It is also an encoding-trick delegation attack surface (C3): an attacker can construct a scope with hidden empty segments to bypass a string-equality check that happens elsewhere.
findings.suggested_fix: Reject any scope containing an empty segment (i.e. reject if any segment after split(':') is empty). Reject scopes starting or ending with ':'. Add unit tests: parse('store::write').is_err(), parse(':store').is_err(), parse('store:').is_err(), parse(':::').is_err().

findings.claim: Documentation claims `CapabilityPolicy::check_read` exists and enforces Phase-1 compromise #2, but no such method is on the trait — the read-capability enforcement surface is absent from the policy trait entirely, and the doc link is broken.
findings.detail: Lib.rs line 22 reads 'Documented on [`CapabilityPolicy::check_read`]' but the CapabilityPolicy trait only has check_write. `cargo doc -p benten-caps --no-deps` emits 'warning: unresolved link to CapabilityPolicy::check_read'. The DeniedRead variant exists in CapError and maps to ErrorCode::CapDeniedRead, but nothing in benten-caps constructs or returns it. This means: (1) the documented compromise #2 (E_CAP_DENIED_READ leaks existence) has no enforcement point in the policy surface; (2) a future UCAN backend has no trait hook to implement read-capability checking; (3) the code path that would fire CapError::DeniedRead exists only in spec text, not in the trait. The implementer's self-report acknowledged this but framed it as 'no R3 test exercised it' — but the presence of DeniedRead in the enum and in ERROR-CATALOG.md makes this a promised-but-undelivered surface, not an unused variant.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a default-method `check_read(&self, ctx: &ReadContext) -> Result<(), CapError> { Ok(()) }` to CapabilityPolicy (default permits, NoAuth keeps default, UCAN stub overrides with NotImplemented) and fix the doc link, OR (b) remove the CapError::DeniedRead variant and the broken doc link until Phase 3 lands the read path. Do not leave a live error code with no code path that fires it — audit pipelines will see 0% coverage on that code and treat it as dead.

findings.claim: WriteContext has no `ops` field or any list of writes-about-to-commit, so check_write operates on a principal/label summary only and cannot make per-write decisions — undermining the pre-write hook's stated purpose.
findings.detail: The hook fires once per commit (confirmed by check_write_called_at_commit.rs) but the context has only a single target_label, a single scope, and an actor hint. A 5-WRITE transaction surfaces as one check_write call with no enumeration of what's being written. This means: (1) a policy CANNOT enforce per-write capability rules (e.g. 'alice can write Post but not AdminSettings' if a transaction contains both); (2) a policy CANNOT detect whether a batch stays within a declared scope; (3) compromise #1 (TOCTOU at batch boundary) is unverifiable from the policy's perspective because the policy never sees what writes landed. The implementer deferred ops wiring to G3, but the pre-write hook contract in ENGINE-SPEC §9 'every WRITE is checked' cannot be honored by a policy that only sees a summary. Worst case: a G3 wiring that passes only metadata (not individual ops) produces a hook that structurally cannot enforce principal-confusion protection against a prompt-injected agent driving N writes under one transaction.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `pub ops: Vec<WriteOp>` to WriteContext where WriteOp is at least `{ target_cid, target_label, op_kind: {PutNode,PutEdge,Delete} }`. Have G3 populate it from the transaction's pending batch. Add a test `check_write_sees_every_pending_write` that fires 5 writes and asserts ctx.ops.len() == 5. Without this the hook is principal-level only, which makes compromise #1's batch-boundary-refresh story vacuous (you can't refuse a specific write at the boundary if you can't see which writes are in flight).

findings.claim: DEFAULT_BATCH_BOUNDARY = 100 is an informational constant with no enforcement in benten-caps itself; there is no contract surface that forces the G6 evaluator to honor it.
findings.detail: The constant is a `pub const usize` with no associated API, no trait method that takes it, no runtime validation. The evaluator in G6 is contractually obligated (per toctou_iteration.rs test) to refresh cap snapshots every 100 iterations, but nothing in benten-caps enforces that. A subtle future refactor in G6 that changes the refresh cadence to 500 or 1000 would not fail any benten-caps test; it would only fail toctou_iteration.rs IF that test hasn't been moved or relaxed. This is a named-compromise regression risk: the documented bound (TOCTOU window ≤100 writes per grant) is enforced by test, not by type. The auditor brief requires 'financial/write caps have TTL ≤5 minutes'; a 100-iteration batch at e.g. 100µs/iter is 10ms of TOCTOU window — fine — but at 1 iter/sec (with an expensive TRANSFORM) it's 100 seconds, far above 5 minutes at batch-3. Nothing guards against this.
findings.suggested_fix: (a) Introduce a trait method `fn max_batch_boundary(&self) -> usize { DEFAULT_BATCH_BOUNDARY }` on CapabilityPolicy so policies can tighten the bound. (b) Add a wall-clock bound alongside the iteration bound — a cap snapshot is valid for min(100 iterations, 5 minutes wall time). (c) Document in the constant that a future backend can override it. Today's 'binding-only' status is brittle.

findings.claim: The auditor success metric 'Attenuation proptest passes over 10,000 random chains' is not met — only three hand-picked attenuation cases exist (one denial, one permit, one NoAuth baseline). No property test generates random parent/child chains and asserts the effective scope is a structural subset of every parent.
findings.detail: Ripgrep across benten-caps for `proptest|attenuation` shows call_attenuation.rs contains only deterministic unit tests. noauth_proptest.rs is a proptest but covers NoAuth's unconditional-permit contract, not attenuation. Given findings g4-uc-1 and g4-uc-2 — where the current algorithm has concrete bypasses at parent='*' and parent='store:*' vs deeper child — a random-chain proptest would very likely detect these within the first 100 generations. The absence of the proptest is both a test-coverage gap AND the reason the bugs escaped detection.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `tests/attenuation_proptest.rs` with a strategy that generates random scope strings (segment counts 1..6, each segment is '*' or a random [a-z]+ literal), random chains of length 2..8, and asserts: for every adjacent pair (parent, child) where check_attenuation(parent, child) succeeds, the child's permitted-set (expanded to concrete scope strings) is a STRUCTURAL subset of the parent's permitted-set. Run 10_000 cases. Expected outcome on current code: the proptest fails on a wildcard-expansion case and drives the fix for g4-uc-1/g4-uc-2.

findings.claim: Two construction paths for CapabilityGrant with different fields — struct-literal path has no issuer, free-function `new` folds issuer into properties only. This is a principal-aliasing vector and a code-review trap, though not a live exploit in Phase 1.
findings.detail: The struct `CapabilityGrant { grantee, scope, hlc_stamp }` has no issuer. `CapabilityGrant::new(grantee, issuer, scope)` returns a `Node` with issuer in the property map. Both paths are public. An unwary consumer that constructs a grant via struct literal, serializes it via as_node(), and feeds it into a check pipeline that expects a grant-node to have an 'issuer' property will see a grant with NO issuer property — effectively an 'unauthenticated root issuer' pass. Today no code in benten-caps itself consumes grant issuers (Phase 3 UCAN does), so this is not a live exploit. But it IS a correctness-by-construction failure: a Phase-3 UCAN backend reading CapabilityGrant Nodes off the graph will encounter grants with and without issuers, and the 'without' case is indistinguishable from a deliberate unauthenticated grant. Grant uniqueness tests (`grant_issuer_difference_changes_cid`) only exercise the new() path — the struct-literal path's CID does NOT depend on issuer, so a well-meaning test-fixture author could unknowingly ship unauthenticated grants.
findings.suggested_fix: (a) Make struct-literal construction private to the crate; expose only `CapabilityGrant::new(grantee, issuer, scope, hlc)` publicly. (b) OR: add an explicit `issuer: Option<Cid>` field to the struct and have as_node() emit the issuer property only when Some; require a Cid even if that Cid is a documented 'self-issued' sentinel. (c) Add a test that a struct-literal-built grant either HAS an issuer property or is rejected at use-site. The implementer's note cites 'test-contract asymmetry' as the driver; consider whether those tests should be updated now rather than grandfathering a principal-confusion vector through Phase 1.

findings.claim: Two error variants (`Denied` unit, `DeniedDetail` struct) map to the same ErrorCode and could let a logging/audit pipeline miss denials that flow through the bare path.
findings.detail: Any CapError::Denied emitted by `GrantScope::parse` or by a test fixture carries no (required, entity) payload, so an audit sink that reads .required / .entity (via match only on DeniedDetail) will silently skip these. The code() fn catches both into ErrorCode::CapDenied, so downstream consumers that only look at the code are fine. But the error Display difference ('capability denied' vs 'capability denied (required=..., entity=...)') could confuse structured log parsers. Not a live exploit — this is a code-hygiene concern that could mask a denial on write but not on audit trail.
findings.suggested_fix: Collapse to one variant: `Denied { required: Option<String>, entity: Option<String> }`. Use Option to preserve the bare form for fixtures that don't care. Update `grant.rs::parse` to emit `Denied { required: None, entity: None }`. Or: document a lint (clippy::pattern_type_mismatch? a doc-comment in error.rs?) requiring all audit-pipeline match arms to match BOTH variants.

findings.claim: The crate ships a STUB_MARKER const that benten-engine pins via `const _: &str = benten_caps::STUB_MARKER;` — implying the crate is NOT production-ready, but no cargo feature or runtime warning prevents a production deploy from shipping stub-phase code.
findings.detail: The doc-comment on STUB_MARKER says 'G8 removes this once the engine-side reference is retired' but there is no feature flag, no cfg gate, no log warning, no builder-time assertion that flags this to operators. A production build of benten-engine today would compile cleanly with STUB_MARKER present. The R1 triage's N5-b builder().production() pathway (flagged in the brief) is G7 scope, not G4 — but G4 ALSO didn't add any precautionary signal (e.g. a tracing::warn!() in NoAuthBackend::new() that 'this backend has no authorization and should not be the default in production'). The auditor brief's MUST list includes 'every grant must chain to a self-issued root or a known verified root' — but NoAuthBackend permits without any chain. For Phase 1 this is fine AS DOCUMENTED, but the absence of any guardrail means the G7 'production' builder has no crate-level API to call into.
findings.suggested_fix: (a) Move STUB_MARKER behind `#[cfg(debug_assertions)]` so release builds see a compile error if anything still depends on it. (b) Add a `NoAuthBackend::warn_if_production()` associated fn that tracing::warn!s — G7 can call it at builder finalize when production mode is detected. (c) Add a `benten_caps::testing::assert_not_production()` helper used by tests. None of these are strictly required for G4 to be correct, but they would close the loop for G7's production builder.

findings.claim: WriteContext has no time-bound field or HLC stamp; cap checks implicitly rely on wall-clock 'current time' if a future policy wants to enforce TTL — violating the auditor's MUST-NOT 'capability check must not depend on wall-clock time without HLC verification'.
findings.detail: The auditor brief explicitly lists 'Must not let a capability check depend on wall-clock time without HLC verification (clock manipulation attack)'. WriteContext has no HLC timestamp, no grant-expiry bound, no way for a policy to read 'is this grant still valid at the HLC of this write?'. Today there is no backend that checks TTL (NoAuth permits all, UCAN stub errors), so this is a future-surface concern, not a live exploit. But the WriteContext shape is being pinned by G3 wiring and by napi bindings; adding fields later is a breaking change. The `actor_cid: Option<Cid>` reservation-for-Phase-3 pattern sets a precedent that could be repeated here.
findings.suggested_fix: Reserve an `hlc_stamp: Option<u64>` field on WriteContext now. Reserve a `capability_grant_cid: Option<Cid>` so the resolved grant is threaded through and a future check can compare grant.hlc_stamp + ttl vs ctx.hlc_stamp. Document in the struct docs that wall-clock MUST NOT be consulted; use the HLC stamp. This aligns with the R1 triage Attribution fix that already adds `actor_cid`, `handler_cid`, `capability_grant_cid` to ChangeEvent + TraceStep — WriteContext should get the same shape for consistency.

findings.claim: Phase 1 has Revoked + RevokedMidEval error variants but no revocation mechanism in benten-caps; revocation is entirely an engine/evaluator concern, leaving benten-caps blind to whether a grant it previously blessed has been revoked.
findings.detail: REVOKED_AT_LABEL is defined (grant.rs:30) but unused — no Revocation Node type, no API to mark a grant revoked, no trait method `CapabilityPolicy::is_revoked(grant_cid)`. All revocation plumbing is deferred to G6 (evaluator). This means: during a check_write call, a policy cannot ask 'has this grant been revoked since I last checked?' — the policy would have to maintain its own revocation cache, duplicating engine state. TOCTOU-between-check-and-use (threat C4) is therefore enforced only at the evaluator level, not at the policy level. A future backend that wants to do its own revocation checking (e.g. UCAN checking revocation lists from a P2P channel) has no trait hook.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a trait method `fn is_grant_revoked(&self, grant_cid: &Cid) -> Result<bool, CapError> { Ok(false) }` with a permissive default. NoAuth returns Ok(false). UCAN stub returns NotImplemented. This gives Phase 3 the hook without breaking Phase 1 defaults. Add a Revocation Node type constructor similar to CapabilityGrant::new so the REVOKED_AT edge has a typed counterparty to point to.

notes: Overall G4 landed the trait shape, the two backend types, the grant Node surface, error-code mapping, and the at-commit firing contract cleanly. The cross-crate compile is green. But the attenuation algorithm — which is the single most security-critical surface in the crate — has two concrete wildcard-bypass cases that a 10_000-case random-chain proptest would detect immediately. The WriteContext lacks both the `ops` list (so the hook cannot do per-write decisions, undermining the stated purpose) and an HLC stamp (so time-bounded caps cannot be enforced without wall-clock). The broken doc link to check_read points to a bigger issue: CapError::DeniedRead exists but there is no code path that fires it. Verdict REVISE (not BLOCK) because: (a) G3 will add `ops` to WriteContext — finding g4-uc-5 is a contract that G3 must honor, and G4 can pre-land the field shape; (b) the attenuation bugs are concentrated in the `testing::` helper and are trivially fixed; (c) NoAuthBackend's behavior is correct for Phase 1. Block-level findings would require the wildcard bypass to ship into the evaluator's CALL attenuation check (G6) — it has not yet. Recommend: fix g4-uc-1/g4-uc-2/g4-uc-3 (attenuation algorithm + parse validation) + g4-uc-4 (remove or implement check_read surface) + g4-uc-7 (add random-chain proptest) before G6 builds on top of this crate. Defer g4-uc-5 to G3 design review with a pre-landed field shape. Defer g4-uc-10/g4-uc-11/g4-uc-12 (builder hardening + HLC reserve + revocation hook) to G7/G8.
